angle.headline: Why Your M&A Diligence Process Finds Everything Except What Kills Deals
angle.thesis: Middle-market owners spend six figures on diligence that audits the past but never diagnoses whether the target can operate without its founder — the single variable that determines post-close performance.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners in growth-through-acquisition mode, particularly those who've completed one deal and are now considering their second, operating with $10M-$75M revenue across the US-LATAM corridor
angle.relevanceToAudience: With USMCA at its six-year review and nearshoring momentum creating acquisition opportunities across the corridor, owners are evaluating targets in Mexico and LATAM — but standard diligence frameworks were built for US-only deals and miss the founder-dependency risk that is magnified across borders and jurisdictions.

peopleAngle.challenge: The seller's leadership team has never operated without the founder's real-time input, and the buyer's integration team lacks the trust or context to make decisions during the 90-day transition window when founder dependency becomes operationally visible.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

justification.angleChoice: Most M&A diligence answers 'Does this business make money?' but never asks 'Can this business make decisions without the seller in the room?' — the real post-close question.
justification.osPillarChoice: The failure happens because diligence is structured to audit the past (financials, compliance, IP) rather than diagnose the operating model's ability to execute independently — particularly across time zones and legal systems in corridor deals.
justification.peopleAngleChoice: The Machine Test: before you sign the LOI, spend one week asking only this: 'Who makes what decisions when the owner is unavailable?' If the answer is 'it waits' or 'it escalates,' you are buying a job, not a business.

entries.fieldReportSummary: [Meta announced this week](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-layoffs-internal-memo-ai-job-cuts-faq-2026-5) that it will cut 10% of staff on Wednesday, eliminating around 8,000 roles in three waves of notifications timed to 4am local time across regions. angle.headline: Why Your Best People Leave When You Finally Fix the System
angle.thesis: Business owners who delay succession planning discover too late that building the operating model that runs without them creates the very conditions that make their successors unwilling to stay.
angle.targetPersona: Founder-owners age 50+ with $10M–$50M revenue who built the business through force of will and now face the paradox that the stronger they make the machine, the less attractive the opportunity becomes to the next generation
angle.relevanceToAudience: The T-MEC revision (items 5, 14) creates urgency for institutional succession planning in corridor businesses, but most owners are discovering that the Operating Model OS they need to scale is being built exactly when their natural successors are evaluating other options—succession isn't failing because owners won't let go, it's failing because they're letting go of the wrong things at the wrong time
talkingPoints: The owner who spent 15 years building systems to run without him now faces a successor who sees a professional management job, not an ownership opportunity—the machine works, but the mission died in the transition

Succession fails when owners confuse delegation (giving away tasks) with development (giving away decisions)—NextGen doesn't want your processes, they want your judgment, and by the time you systematize judgment you've removed the reason they wanted the business

The Kotter mistake: most owners skip Step 2 (building the guiding coalition) and jump to Step 5 (removing obstacles)—they build the operating model before they build the ownership team, so there's no one left who wants to own what they built

The corridor dimension: Latin American family businesses that succeed multi-generationally don't wait until the machine is perfect—they transfer equity and authority early, messily, while the business still needs the successor's instinct, not just their execution

peopleAngle.challenge: The owner must transfer decision-making authority (not just operational responsibility) early enough that the successor still sees an ownership opportunity worth the risk, which requires the owner to trust an incomplete leader with an incomplete system—the opposite of how they built the business
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

entries.fieldReportSummary: Yum Brands sold Pizza Hut to LongRange Capital for $1.5 billion this week, with Yum China separately acquiring the Mainland business for $1.2 billion, according to [Middle Market Growth](https://middlemarketgrowth.org/pe-weekly-pizza-hut-take-private-tech-deals/).
